title: Chip design from the bottom up – Reiner Pope

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I'm back with Reiner Pope who is the CEO of Madix, which is a new AI chip company. Last time we were talking about what happens inside a data center. Now I want to understand what happens inside an AI chip. How does a chip actually work? Full disclosure, by the way, I am an angel investor in Madix, um, so hopefully you have designed a good chip.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So I'll start with sort of the very smallest fundamental unit of of of chip design and then we'll sort of build up into what an overall like actual production chip, what are the components of that.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: At the very bottom level of a chip, the primitives that we work with are uh logic gates, um which are very simple things like and or not, um and then these are connected together by by wires that have to be laid out uh physically as metal traces on a chip.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: The main function that that AI chips want to compute is uh multiplication of matrices and really inside that is the fundamental primitive is multiply-accumulate just like of of pairs of numbers.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So we're going to sort of demonstrate what that calculation looks like by hand and then sort of infer what what a uh circuit would look like for that.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: It'll turn out to be sort of easiest if I do multiplication uh accumulate something like a um a four-bit number um with another four-bit number, um and then we're going to the the actual clearest primitive is actually um multiply-accumulate so there's a multiply these two terms and then we're going to add in so product of these two terms and then we're going to add in an eight-bit uh number.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And and can I ask a clarifying question? Why is this the um natural primitive for um you know whatever computation happens inside a computer?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so um there's a few reasons for this. Um it it's a little bit more efficient um but the the reason it's natural for AI chips is that if you look what's happening during a matrix multiply, the uh what is matrix multiply in very short? It is um uh there's a for loop over I and over J and over K

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: of output I K plus equals to uh input I J times other input J K.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so multiply-accumulate happens at every single step of of a matrix multiply.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Makes sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And then the other observation is that um the precision will almost always be higher in the accumulation step than in the multiplication step. Um this is maybe specific to AI chips uh but you're you're multiplying low precision numbers but then when you accumulate uh errors accumulate quickly and so you need more precision here. So this is why we've chosen to do a four-bit multiplication and an eight-bit addition.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Let me make sure I understood that.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: There's two ways to understand that, one is that the value will be larger than the inputs, and the other is that if it was a floating point number it would be maybe that that part is like less intuitive to me, but it's maybe the same principle.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: It is really the same principle. Um, I guess the so I mean I guess the separate principle is that as you are summing up this number, you are summing up a whole bunch of numbers, and so you've got a lot of rounding errors accumulating, whereas in this case, there's like there's only one multiplication in that chain, and so there's not a lot of rounding errors accumulating in the multiplication.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Why are you summing up a whole bunch of numbers, it's just two numbers?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Just I mean, this summation happens it's repeated j... j... times and so...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Oh I see I see I see I see so yeah any errors accumulate I see yeah I see.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, how would we perform this calculation by hand? I mean as a human we would probably separate it into two, but we can sort of do it all all in one um using long multiplication. So the multiplication term first, we're going to multiply this number, this 4-bit number here, by every single bit position in the other 4-bit number. So we write that out. Um, first 1001 multiplied by this bit position... that is this number itself. Then shifted across by one, we're multiplying by zero... that gives us an all zeros number. Shifted um across even one more to multiply by this one... we get 1001. And then finally for this last last bit position, we get another all zeros number again. So this this sort of gives us a bunch of terms that we're going to have to add for the um for the multiplication. And then while we're doing that uh summation of this, we might as well add in the the actual accumulator term as well and so we just copy that directly across. So, so this is the sum, it's a it's a 5-way sum that we're going to want to compute.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So firstly, what what logic gates did it take us to even get to this intermediate step? We needed to uh produce all 16 of these partial products.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mm-hm.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: How do I produce one of these partial products? So let's take this this number one for example here, it is one, so what was how do we produce this number by multiplying this number by this one over here?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: We can actually produce that by an AND gate, this this number is one if both this bit is one and this bit is one. If either of them is zero, then the multiplication of one times one um of zero times anything is zero. So to produce all of this stuff, we ended up uh consuming 16 AND gates. Or in the general case, if I were doing a like a P-bit multiply times a Q-bit multiply...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Then this will be like P times Q many ANDs.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Finally I sum them. Actually, most of the work is going to happen in the summing. Um, and so let me describe, uh, sort of the other logic gate that we use here. AND is almost the simplest logic gate that exists on a chip. It's, it's, it's almost the smallest. Um, at the other extreme, typically the very largest logic gate that you'll use is something called a full adder.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And what this does is sort of, it does, like, coming from software, you might think that a full adder adds 32-bit numbers together. In this case, it just adds three single-bit numbers together. And so you can think of it as like adding 0, 1, and 1 together. Now, when I add these together, the net result can be 0, 1, 2, or 3. So I can express that in binary using just two bits. So it, it, as input it has three bits and as output it has two bits, which in this case are 1 and, like, the number 2 in binary is 1-0.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So so this is also known as a three-to-two compressor because it takes three bits of input and produces two bits of output.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The two inputs are an X and a Y value and then some carry that was- that came in from like-

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: The- sorry, the the three inputs are uh, all bits that are in the same sort of bit position, like three bits that are in a column here. Yeah, okay. And then the two outputs, um, I have sort of drawn them vertically here and horizontally here to kind of match this vertical versus horizontal layout here, which is expressing that um, things that are in the same column are in the same like bit position, um, whereas things that are in adjacent columns, like this is a carry out, whereas this was a sum.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So if the inputs in the full adder are, let's say, like 1-0-1, then the output will still be 1-0. If it was 1-1-1, it'd be 1-1. If it was 0-0-0, it'd be 0-0. If it was like 0-1-0, it'd still be 0-1. So yeah, it's just counting essentially the number of, uh, number of things and expressing that in binary.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So this circuit actually can sort of capture what we as humans naturally do, um, when we're, when we're doing summing along a column. So, uh, I can show that sort of, I'll show sort of one iteration of using the full adder to sum. Um, the way I sum here is going to be a little bit unnatural for humans. Humans would sort of sum along the column and then remember the carry, um, but, uh, instead of remembering the carry, we'll actually just explicitly write it out. So in this we proceed from the rightmost column towards the left. On the rightmost column, we sum the 1 and the 1 and that produces like a, a 0 here and a carry of 1. So we've, we've sort of used a, this full adder circuit on this pair of bits and produced a pair of bits as output. Now we can do the-

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Same thing with this column, we've got a column of one, two, three, four numbers, and so maybe we'll like take the first three of them, run a full adder on them, and that gives us a zero and a zero as output. Um, so like the sum of these is zero-zero. And so that's the full compressor full adder applied to all these bits. As I've used up bits, I'm going to sort of just cross them out to indicate that I've I've handled them.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Let's just keep going a little more, so we'll we'll go here. I take these three numbers, I add them, that gives me a one and a zero. I've dealt with these three numbers. And now I take one, two, and I can even take these three numbers, for example, right now, and add them, and that gives me a one and a zero and I've dealt with these numbers.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, I can sort of like the way I should view this is that I have this whole grid of numbers that need to be added. I'm going to just keep applying full adders to all the bits that are here, constantly removing three numbers from a column and then writing out two numbers as output. Um, keep going with this over and over and over and again until I eventually get like some just one single number coming out here. Something like that. Uh, this is probably the wrong sum.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, so this approach that I've described here, this is called a Dadda multiplier. Um, and this is sort of like the standard for how how you do area efficient multiplies using full adders.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Let's try and quantify the circuit size of this just so we've got a sense of like how big things are so we can compare to them later. How many full adders do I did I use? I started with how many numbers? I have the 16 partial products, which is the product of all of these terms with all of these terms, plus the eight terms that I'm adding here. So I I started off with 24 bits.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And then on I produced eight bits on the output eventually. And in every step I was sort of crossing off three numbers and writing two numbers out as in result. And so every single use of a full adder eliminates one of the bits here. And so how many full adders it must be the 24 minus the eight, so that there were 16 full adders in this circuit. In general, this is true in the general case as well, there will be P times Q many full adders in this circuit.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Let me just make sure I understand the logic of that. So the input bits 24 is P times Q plus P plus Q.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And the output bits is just P plus Q. And so P times Q plus P plus Q minus P plus Q equals P times Q.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So I think this explains...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: sort of or at least hints at the second reason why we chose to do a multiply accumulate. First reason being that's actually what shows up in matrix multiplication, but second reason being it gave us this very slick P times Q, very simple algebra.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mhmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So we've sort of described like this this whole procedure, every single atomic step that I took here becomes a logic gate and then sort of the wires connected together, like when I had these three inputs that I summed to produce these two outputs. Like if I think of mapping this to a physical device, they would be a wire that runs sort of connecting all three of these things together into a logic gate that produced this output. Okay, so this is the the main primitive at different bit widths um that that is that is inside an AI chip. Um we're going to build up from here to how would you use that to run all of the other operations you might want.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: This may be the wrong time to ask this question, but whenever NVIDIA reports that this chip can do X many FP4 or half as many FP8, it seems to imply that those circuits are fungible. Uh that there's not as dedicated like FP4 versus FP8 um but the way you're mapping it out here, it seems like you would need if it has to be mapped out in the logic, you would need a dedicated FP4 multiply accumulate and a dedicated FP8 accumulate. Basically, can you can you funge them?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: As drawn, they're actually not particularly fungible. Um this is actually one of the main choices you have to make when designing a chip, which is how much of FP4, how much of FP8 do I have. And then at sometimes they'll make that consideration from the point of view of like what do I think the customer requirement, another way to take an angle on that is to say what is the what is the power budget for equalize the power budget between FP4 and FP8.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But so then when they report those numbers and they just happen to be the case that like it does 2x as many FP4 as FP8, they just happen to choose like give the equivalent die areas to all the floating points and as a result that ended up being like why's the ratio exactly 2x?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so part of it is, I mean, the surely that won't be exactly like exactly equivalent die area. There's a data movement reason actually, and we will maybe come back to this when we sort of look through how it goes into and out of memory. There's something really nice just from a software level of the fact that I can pack two 4-bit numbers into the same storage as an 8-bit number, and so when I store that to a memory or something like that, it the the sizing of the the buses that I wire within the chip actually makes that work out really nicely.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Actually, come to think of it, it's not just 2x, it would be the amount of area it takes, it sounds like is quadratic in fact with the bit length, so that's why smaller precision is like-

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: be more favorable than the United States. This is a really big reason.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, in fact Nvidia made a change, historically, up until B100 or B200 every time you have the bit precision you double the flop count.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: That ratio is exactly like for the reason you said because of this quadratic scaling that ratio is actually slightly wrong. It should be like an even bigger, you should get an even bigger speed up than you than you might otherwise think.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Nvidia's, like, product specs have sort of started acknowledging that in B300 and beyond where the FP4 is three times faster than the FP8.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Though it should be 4x.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah. What I've shown here is like the simplest case of integer multiply. When you're when you're dealing with floating point as you do in FP4 and FP8, there's this sort of other term which is the the exponent that just complicates this this calculation.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So, so what can we see already from this? I think the the big observation you've made is that there's this quadratic scaling with bit width which which is like very effective and and is the single reason why low precision arithmetic has worked so well for neural nets.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: But the other thing we're going to do now is compare sort of the area spent on the multiplication itself with all the circuitry that is around it. So we'll walk back in time a little bit and see how did GPUs prior to tensor cores work? As which is the same way as the way that CPUs worked in fact. So which is like where do we stick this multiply accumulate unit? So generically I'll describe like a CUDA core or a CPU, you'll have some register file which stores some number of entries, maybe it's like eight entries of, in this case I guess four bit numbers, but typically like 32 bit numbers or something like that of just numbers. So this is the like inside the CUDA core, I'll have some register file of some depth and then I will have my multiply accumulate circuit, multiply and accumulate circuit. And what it's going to do, it's going to like it's going to take three arbitrary registers from this register file, perform the multiply accumulate and then write back to the register file. So it's going to maybe write to this one, but it was able to read from this one, this one and another random one. So it'll take three inputs like this. So this is the core data path of many processors, most processors look like this. You've got some set of registers and then you've got some set of logic units or ALUs. We want to analyze the cost of the data movement from the register file to to the ALU and back. So ultimately there's going to be some circuit that says well I don't always

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Just like this guy, I might select any of the registers at any point in time. And so, sort of a first question is, how can I build a circuit? The circuit that I'm going to look for is a MUX. So, in this case, it's going to have eight inputs, one from each entry of the register file, and it's going to have one output, which is actually producing this output.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And then, like, what is the cost of this thing? It's like all we have to build it out of is AND and OR. And so how do we build it? We do the dumbest thing possible. We like form a mask saying, okay, when we want to read like the third entry, we're going to AND every single entry with either one or zero based on whether that's what we want to read, and then we're going to OR all of them together.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, just to make sure I understand the basics. What the MUX is doing is it's just like selecting, just selecting an input.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so like invisible to software, it's like you say I want input number three, that means there's a MUX.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so like, what is the cost of this MUX? So, an n-input MUX operating on p bits... well... I am going to... so I have n rows, that's this eight rows, and I've got like each row is p bits wide. Well, I have to AND every single bit. So I get n times p many AND gates.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Every single input, I have to say, am I going to like mask it out or not? And then... and then I'm going to OR them all together. And so, there's going to be like (n-1) times p many OR gates. Which is saying, I've got all of these different things, almost all of them are zeros, but I need to sort of collapse them down into... into... like from my eight options down into one option. And so every step I need to OR like one row into... into an existing row.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Got it, yeah. It's actually kind of funny that you would sort of... you don't think at the level of hardware, you sort of just think like, oh, I'll just select element three, and something as simple as that is sort of like, in and of itself, a quite complicated circuit.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, this is the first step of all of the hidden data movement costs that show up. Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um, and so like, the thing... like, we're just going to like compare... like I have to pay this cost, and I've got one MUX here, and then in fact I have two more copies of that for each of the three inputs to my multiply-accumulate operation. And so I have this cost which is like, like three times n times p AND gates over here... um, compared to this p times q... like, sort of gates in the actual circuit that... that is doing the thing I care about.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And if we plug in actual numbers, like this n being eight... like I get like twenty-four times p gates over... just... just in the data movement compared to, if q is four, like four times p.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Eight gates just in the, in the adder, multiply-adder.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And sorry, where's the three coming from?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Three different inputs here.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Got it, okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, the point, like really just what I'm hinting at here is that, like, all of this work, which scales as, as the size of the register file, and this is a very small register file, all of this work just moving the data from the register file to the, to the logic unit, um, is many many times more expensive than the logic unit.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: In the most recent Cloud Spectator report, SemiAnalysis ranked almost 100 different GPU clouds. Crusoe was one of only five that made the Gold Tier. SemiAnalysis found that Gold Tier providers like Crusoe had a total cost of ownership that was 5 to 15% lower than Silver Tier ones, even when they had identical GPU pricing. This makes sense because total cost of ownership is downstream of a bunch of different things that don't necessarily show up in the sticker price, but that Crusoe has optimized. Things like how well you detect faults and how quickly you replace failed nodes. For example, Crusoe was one of the first clouds to adopt NV-Sentinel, NVIDIA's own GPU monitoring and self-healing software for enhanced GPU uptime, utilization, and reliability. This lets Crusoe make use of everything that NVIDIA has learned about why chips fail across all their different fleets and deployments, so that Crusoe can catch faults earlier in the process. And once they identify a failure, Crusoe can swap in a healthy node in less than 10 minutes. Because they are not running bare metal, Crusoe doesn't have to spend time installing an operating system or configuring drivers. They can just spin up a new VM on an already running and pre-qualified host. If you want to learn more about this or the other reasons that Crusoe made Gold Tier, go to crusoe.ai/dwarkesh.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It might be helpful to just see what a mux looks like, maybe like a two-bit or a four-bit mux.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, great. So we'll take some inputs, we'll have maybe, like, we'll just do a two-way mux. So we've got, um, two different numbers, we've got these two inputs, um, that are being selected between. And then we have a selector, which says, which can either be, like, 'I want this one,' or, or it could be, 'I want the other one.' So this is a one-hot encoding. Um, so this is what we all start with. And then we, the output we want to produce... like, let's focus on this case, so this is the actual input we got.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: We just want to produce this guy as the, as the result. And so, like, sort of very laboriously what we do is we AND this bit with all of these. And so, um, that produces, like, ANDing this bit with this row. And likewise, we AND this bit with this row. That produces all zeros. So this was the...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: There's four ANDs here. There's four ANDs here. And then finally, we just OR these two together, that gives a one. We OR these two together, that gives a one. We OR these two together, that gives a zero. We OR these two together, that gives a one. And so this is the four ORs.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So like, this actually ends up looking a little bit like addition, in fact, like we did exactly the set of same same set of ANDs here. Uh, so we've added all of these things together, but then instead of collapsing it by using these full adder circuits, we just get a very simple collapsing with OR gates.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And how but I guess that doesn't look like n times p?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um, so, yes, so this was with n equals two inputs.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Ah, right.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: In the general case, we will have n like and sorry, this is n rows, and then we'll have p bits per row. Um, so that gives us the the n by n times p many AND gates.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So this circuit I've described here, almost all of the cost, like uh, seven-eighths of the cost, uh, is in the reading and writing the register file, and only a tiny fraction of the cost is in the logic unit itself. So this is the problem to solve.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um, this this essentially was the state of play, um, prior to the Volta generation of Nvidia GPUs. This is what what this kind of thing is what was inside the CUDA cores, um, and this and this sort of problem statement is uh, what motivated the introduction of tensor cores, which are more generically called systolic arrays.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So sort of the evolution was like uh we had baked this much into hardware uh in this stage. This single line is a multiply-accumulate and this single thing was baked into hardware. The idea of a systolic array is to sort of go two levels of loop up and bake this entire loop out here into hardware. And so the idea being that if we have a much bigger granularity fixed function piece of logic, maybe the taxes we pay on the input and output are much smaller.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It sounds like you're suggesting that if you have um if you go up one step in the uh in the matrix multiply loop that there's some you can tilt the balance more towards compute than communication.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right. Sort of there's two effects that we're going to take advantage of here. One is just that we can do more stuff before per every trip through a register file. Um, and then the other thing we're going to take advantage of is in fact um um in in some of this loop we can take advantage of for example...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: some things staying fixed.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, visually we're going to look at this matrix multiplication. So, this portion of the loop corresponds to a matrix vector multiplication, in fact. So we'll take a matrix and multiply it by a vector.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So how do we do this? We take every column, get multiplied by the vector, and then summed. So we're going to sum sort of along columns. And so this 0 and 3 gets multiplied by the 3 and 7 and gets summed, and then the 1 and 2 gets multiplied by the 3 and 7 and gets summed.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So there is a multiplier-accumulate associated with every single one of these entries in the matrix. So we'll just draw out these four multiplier-accumulates.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Just to make sure I understand why there's four multiply accumulates. So if each entry in the column that corresponds to the output vector is a dot product, in this case it'll be like two multiplications and then the addition of those two multiplications. So like you're accumulating.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so the addition. So really there's only one addition per dot product, but we like to include the initialization of zero. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So what we're going to aim for is to have, so we've got, we want to have quadratically more compute, which we do. We have we've got sort of x times y as much compute as we had before.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: But we're going to want to somehow aim for having only x times as much communication. And this is sort of the intention so that we get this advantage term going as y.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So we've laid down the multiplications.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Bringing in like we're going to want to bring in a vector of size two and so that's sort of already is in line with our coms target. That's fine.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so the idea is that in an AI context, this matrix is actually going to stay fixed for a long period of time.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so instead of like bringing it in from the outside, so we've got some register file sitting over here, we don't want to have the amount of stuff coming out of this register file, this is the term that we want to go as x in some sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: We don't want to bring this full matrix in from the register file every cycle because we don't have enough, that would cost too much in terms of wiring from the register file.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so instead we're going to store, our key trick is that this matrix can be stored locally to the systolic array.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so, um, where we'll store these numbers 0, 1, 2, and 3

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: in in just like a gate called a register that like physically stores these numbers and we're going to reuse these numbers uh over and over again for a large number of different matrix-vector multiplications.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so the optimization here is that like the nature of matrix multiplication is you can store this like square or quadratic thing directly where the logic is happening. full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right. I mean this is the nature of what a matrix multiplication is is that you do a lot of multiplication to get one value out like a dot product is the result of a lot of multiplications and so that optimization means that you can stuff a lot of like multiplication in before you get some value out of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: That's right. That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So like just to complete the picture here of of concretely how that looks, um uh I swapped the three and the two here, um three and two. So somehow we're going to feed a three and a seven in here.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: These participate in sort of this feeds into this multiplication and also feeds into this multiplication, likewise the three feeds into here and also into here and then we're going to sum uh sort of along here with like starting at the top of a column we feed in zeros and then coming out the bottom uh we get results coming out. So sort of just to visually see what we've got, um there's a dot product that is performed along columns in a matrix and that that sort of maps exactly to what is done spatially in the systolic array here. Um so this is one dot product summed vertically and this is a second dot product also summed vertically.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um and then what is the data that needs to go into and out of the register file? We have x amount of data that's coming out here on the output um and we also have sort of um this data coming from the input, x amount of data from the input.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um and so with respect to the input and output vectors at least, um we we've sort of met our goal of having only x as as much data going in and out of the the register file.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: This leaves open the question of like I said that the weight matrices, weight matrix is stored locally in systolic array, how did it get there in the first place? Um is sort of a like at some point you need to boot your chip and populate this data and so where did that come from? The trick is...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Just we just we do it very slowly. So we we very slowly trickle-feed it in into the systolic array. The sort of the simplest strategy is that we we sort of run this daisy chain that says like feed a number into here and then and then on the next clock cycle it'll move down to the next entry of the systolic array. And so we can do that in every in every column in parallel and that gives us sort of... this is also going to come from here and this is going to be another factor of approximately X units of of bandwidth coming in.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay can you just would you mind repeating that sentence one more time?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So like we're sort of like we know that we're going to be bringing in numbers only rarely into the matrix. Yeah. And so we just want to come up with any construction at all such that the amount of wiring that actually feeds into sort of crosses this boundary of the systolic array like this boundary right here we just want to keep that bounded to X and not be not go as XY. And so a particularly simple strategy is that we we sort of bring in a a number into the top row of the systolic array that's what we can do in one clock cycle and then for like for Y consecutive clock cycles we're going to be bringing in the top row every time and then sort of shift all of the other rows down by one. And that keeps the the sort of the wiring that needs to come from this expensive register file only down to a factor of X rather than XY.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I see. Okay. So there's two questions in terms of communication. There's like communication time and then there's communication bandwidth. Yes. And you're saying since we're only going to be loading this in once let's maximize let's minimize bandwidth because bandwidth equals die area and let's just like load it in slowly over like smaller lanes because we're just going to keep this value in there for a while.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting. So it's interesting to me that when we were talking last time about inference across many chips the big high-level thing we were trying to optimize for is increase the amount of compute per memory bandwidth that is to say per communication. And here also we're trying to increase the amount of like actual multiplies or actual additions relative to transporting information from registers to the logic. So in both cases you're trying to maximize compute relative to communication.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Um this this turns up sort of all the way up and down the stack. Um this is sort of close to the bottom sort of like to the gates. Um there's sort of a version that's maybe even closer to the gates of just like even the precision of number format that you choose to use. Um we saw that same effect. There's like a square cube law or a like squared versus linear term going on both in just purely the precision of this ALU but then also in in terms of the size of this the matrix.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So um so this unit is sort of the next bigger unit. We had like the multiplication circuit and then on top of that we have...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: like a pretty large systolic array. I drew it as 2x2, but in like for example older TPUs they would describe it as 128 by 128 of this circuit shown here. And this circuit ends up being... this is the most efficient known mechanism for circuit for implementing a matrix multiply.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I see. So we've talked about sort of... it seems obvious that you should try to maximize compute relative to communication. What are non-obvious trade-offs that actually you know keep you up at night about should we do X or should we do Y? It is not obvious what the answer is.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so, I mean I think most of the decisions in chip design are sizing decisions. And so, already in what we've drawn so far, like... so AI chips all have this circuit in it. They have a systolic array and then somewhere near it a register file providing inputs and outputs. The two sort of... like even within this scope sizing questions that you have are how big should I make my systolic array and how big should I make the register file. And then the trade-off for the size of the systolic array actually these two questions are coupled is... one way to think of it is to say I'm going to like have a budget for how much what percentage of my chip area I want to spend on data movement. So maybe I just say that I want this to be 10% and the systolic array to be 90%. And then sort of I can size my register file. Bigger register files are more flexible, they allow me to run sort of more... I can get more application level performance out, but then they sort of take away from the area spent on the systolic array.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Makes sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Where does the clock cycle of a chip come in and what determines what that is?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And what is a clock cycle of a chip?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, I guess at baseline, it's sort of worth observing that chips are incredibly, incredibly parallel, right? You've got 100 billion transistors in a chip. A key thing that you need to do whenever you have, like, massive parallelism is you need to synchronize between the different parallel units. In software typically you like you you have these very expensive synchronization methods like a a mutex so one thread will finish what it's doing it will in like it will go grab a lock somewhere stored in memory and then notify the other thread that it's done. On chips we take a very different approach and say that every like nanosecond or so all all circuitry in the chip will kind of pause for a moment and then and then synchronize every so it actually synchronizes every single nanosecond or so.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so that is the clock cycle. The entire chip typically all in sort of one fell swoop goes in lockstep to the next operation that happens. And so what this looks like in circuitry is that you will have this is typically drawn so the clock is sort of mediated by registers which are these storage devices that we've drawn elsewhere. And the way to think of it is that I have I have some storage which is storing like a bit which might be zero or one. And then I have some sort of cloud of logic which may be is like this systolic array or this multiplier or something like that. And then I've got some and that's going to produce some output. So my inputs I've got a bunch of inputs feeding through this cloud of logic and then eventually later there's going to be some output register that this writes to.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: There is a global clock signal which drives all of these registers. And it says at a certain instance in time when the clock strikes whatever value happens to be on this wire at that instant, that's what's going to get stored in there. And so the sort of the challenge here is like I would like to have my clock speed run as fast as possible because if I can run at 2 gigahertz I can get twice as many operations done per per second than if I run at 1 gigahertz. But what that ends up meaning is that I am very sensitive to the delay through this cloud of logic because any computation that is going to happen in here needs to sort of finish before the next clock cycle hits.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So a major point of sort of optimization on any chip then is is to make is to sort of make this delay delay from here as short as possible.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting. And is there ever okay so because the constraint here seems to be that if you...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: If you add too much logic, then you might risk missing the clock cycle. But if you don't add enough, then you're, you know, leaving potential compute on the table. Is there ever a situation where you're like you'd take a probabilistic chance that a computation finishes, and or is it just like, no, it either is going to finish by clock cycle or not?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, in standard chip design, you imagine it such that, I mean, there is a probability, but it's like many, many standard deviations, like way standard deviations out such that for all intents and purposes, it is a reliable part. It will always meet the clock. There are some weird exceptions to that. There are clock domain crossings where you go from one clock to the other clock, and then you actually do have to reason about this probability. In the main path, you just like, you imagine it such that you'll get there like 25% of the clock cycle in advance, so that it's very unlikely.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And in this um the clock uh where the clock synchronize, I guess where the registers are, this is not something you determine as a chip designer, this is sort of just like an artifact of, hey, I want whatever sequence of logic and then the software you use to convert your Verilog into the thing you send to TSMC, that just determines like, hey, in order to make this work, you gotta put a register here, here, and here, to make sure that there's a there's no one step that is like too long such that it makes the whole clock cycle of the entire chip longer than it has to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so this is actually a huge part of the work of of designing a chip actually, is inserting them. So it is done in a combination of manually and automatically. So I mean, like just like to show the very sort of dumb version of like what you can do here, you can take this logic and split it in half, and so like say, actually instead of just one cloud of logic, I'm gonna have two smaller clouds of logic, which do the same thing, but split them up by a register feeding in like this. And this has like, like if you split it like in the middle, you can hit twice the clock frequency. That's great, you get twice the performance at the cost of this extra register, and so at the cost of some more storage.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so stepping back, why do we need to synchronize the whole chip? Like if you have like if you imagine playing Factorio or something, there's no like global clock cycle, it just shit is done when it's done, there's iron on the plate, you can take it if you want.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so um taking that analogy, um the the the thing that you need to be mindful of is if I've got two different paths through some logic. So I have to do a computation like F here, and then computation G here, and then they're going to come and meet for computation H somewhere here. And so there's going to be manufacturing variance here. In some chips...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: F will take a little longer maybe in some chips, G will take a bit a little bit longer. And so if I've got some signal that's propagating through here, um and the result from F and G have to sort of meet up at H, um what can the- the thing that can go wrong is that F can get there early and it meets like the previous value of G or the next value of G or something like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And H needs to know when to start.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Like when- when has this next iteration of- and so this explains why different chips made at the same process node, at the same like TSMC uh technology, uh can have different clock cycles. Like a- two chips made at three nanometer might have different clock cycles based on uh whether they were able to optimize making sure that like there's no one critical path that is so long that um it- it slows down the whole chip's clock cycle.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right. This optimization that I- that I showed here, this is just the- this is sort of um pipeline register insertion, it's called. Um we've inserted in the middle of the pipeline the register here. Um this is a sort of pure trade-off between clock speed and- and area. Yeah. Um this is the easy case. There is a harder case too, which is um- so I've drawn it as a pipeline of logic here, but in other cases you may have some um calculation which actually feeds back in on itself. So, uh it runs some function F and then writes back to uh itself like this.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mhm. So for example, this might be this addition? Like you've got some number that you're adding to- into every clock cycle. And so this- this could be like a- this could be like a plus, uh where adding in some number every clock cycle.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Mhm. So this- like this little circuit, um essentially it's just going to sum all of the numbers that you presented on different clock cycles. And the challenge is if this plus takes too long, what can I do? If I like split it in- if I try and put a pipeline register like right in the middle of it, um like right here in the middle of it, um this will end up changing the computation that's done. Instead of forming a running sum of everything that comes here, I will actually have two different running sums. I'll end up having a running sum of the even numbers and a running sum of the odd numbers. So (sort of) this constraint where I have um a loop in my logic, which all chips have somewhere, um this is actually the thing that is the hardest thing to- to address and- and sets the clock cycle.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I don't understand why it would be a problem to have that. Or I'm not sure even what it would mean to have a- register there. Like it's a sort of- atomic operation, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well so plus is not really atomic, uh like I think- as you just demonstrated. Yeah, yeah, it took a whole lot of work to do a- summation. And so like you can take- the early parts of that work and then- and then stick a register in the middle and then do- take the late parts of that work.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. Okay. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And I guess it's then up to- so TSMC offers a- PDK which says, hey, here's the primitives of- logic that we can grant you.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And in the chip, and it's up to them to determine that no primitive is bigger than like the clock cycle they're hoping a process node targets. But other than that, is there like what further optimization? Can't you just say like, hey, here's all the primitives from TSMC and if keep adding registers in between the primitives as much as is needed until you get to your desired clock cycle?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, as a logic designer, like the chip architects set the clock cycle. So just for one example,

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: the primitives you get from TSMC are on the order of like AND gates or full adders. They depends a lot on voltage and frequency and which library you choose and so on. But generally they, you can typically have about like 10 or 20 or 30 of these in a clock cycle sequentially. So these primitives are very, very fast, like 10 picoseconds or something like that. And so as a logic designer, I mean, like in principle if you literally just had like register and then AND gate, kind of in a loop like that, you could get an insanely fast clock speed, like more than 4 or 5, 6 GHz, something like that. But if you take this like really sort of simple circuit and you look at the area you're spending here, like this is maybe like one, I mean this is called one gate equivalent in size. So like unit of one in area, and this thing is like unit of eight in area or something like that. And so like this is just almost again, almost all of your cost has been this like synchronization or communication cost compared to the actual logic. And so this would be a case where you've gone too far. You've made your clock speed really, really fast at the cost of spending almost all of your area on pipeline registers.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting. So what you're hinting at is a dynamic where you can have really fast clock speed, but you're not getting that much work done. And so you can have like low latency, but low bandwidth.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Or throughput rather. Yeah, the throughput of your chip you can think of as the product of how much do I get done per clock cycle, which is based on this area efficiency thing, times how many clocks I get per second. This is actually so similar to the thing we were discussing last time about like batch size, where if you have a low batch size, then you can, any one user can receive their next token really fast, but the total number of tokens that are processed in say an hour will be kind of lower than it could otherwise be. Yeah, exactly. You get less parallelism out if you drive your clock speed up really high.
