full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, so I remember talking to an FPGA engineer at Jane Street, Clark, who actually helped me prep for your, the previous interview we did together, and he was explaining why they use FPGAs and I imagine that for high frequency trading, throughput is less important than latency. And so having very specific control over the clock cycle in a deterministic way is the most important thing. I, maybe it'd be interesting to talk about how you, why you can't just achieve that with an ASIC or why FPGA is the, yeah why you might use an FPGA to have deterministic clock cycles for like high frequency trading.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I mean, firstly like let's consider the sort of the business case for an FPGA versus an ASIC. FPGAs and ASICs use largely the same sort of conceptual model, which is that I have a series of gates built from ANDs, ORs, XORs, those very small primitives, connected together with a fixed clock cycle and connected together with wires that are running in a fixed clock cycle. So anything you can express in an FPGA, you can express in an ASIC too, and it'll be about an order of magnitude cheaper and like better energy efficiency on an ASIC than an FPGA. The tradeoff is that the first FPGA costs you ten thousand dollars, whereas the first ASIC you make costs you thirty million dollars because of... it requires an entire tape out. So sort of the business use case for an FPGA would be that I want something that has this very deterministic latency and fast runtime and high parallelism, but I'm going to change it very frequently, change what I do every month or something like that, and so then I don't want to pay the tape out cost every time. Now, how does an FPGA actually implement... it sort of emulates the ASIC programming model but in a fixed piece of hardware. And so how does that work actually? So what it has at the base is it's got the two components we just talked about. It's got these registers as storage devices and then it's got these...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: These are called LUTs, look-up tables, which actually provide all of the gates.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So, and then, and then we're going to see in the sort of the third component, we then have like sort of a swarm of these registers and LUTs and all of these are available and then they are connected by sort of this big set of—

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Muxes. So in front of every single one of these we've got something like one of these muxes which selects one input from everywhere else, sort of selecting from all of these different... you know, we've got a whole bunch of different options feeding into into into all of these things.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So what this allows is essentially a, when I program my FPGA I can say that I'm going to take all of these components and I'm going to sort of superimpose on top of this a particular wiring which like goes through this LUT and then feeds into this LUT and then goes to this register and then feeds into this LUT or something like that. So what I've drawn in orange is like how you... like FPGA means field programmable gate array. This is the orange is what has been programmed in the field whereas the white is all of the wires that must exist in the FPGA in order to actually take to make the device in the first place.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What does it mean to be programmed into a field?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Like programmed in the field, so like the device has been deployed in a data center, it's sitting in the field and then you can come and program it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Not field as in like electromagnetic field or—

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Field as in like out there in the world field. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so if I see look at the how the the field programming comes out of the first lookup table and goes into a second one, how is it... how... yeah, how? Like like where are the wires that make that happen I guess? Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So I I got a little bit like lazy in drawing all of these... every single device here has a mux sitting in front of it which can select from all of the nearby like circuits that are available. And so the actual configuration of the FPGA is like amounts to it is the mux control. So like we in this mux here we have the data inputs and then we have like the control that selects. And so like there's a little storage device sitting next to every single one of these muxes saying this is where you're going to source your input from.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Got it.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so programming it consists of like configuring every single one of these muxes.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So that makes sense. What is happening inside the look up table?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So the purpose of the look up table, so it's going to also have a little bit of control feeding telling it what to do as well. The purpose of the look up table is to function to—

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: be able to configurably take the role of an AND gate, OR gate, XOR, any of those different things. So there's many ways you could consider doing that. The way it is done in sort of traditional FPGAs is to say it will support, so it will be a lookup table will be, it will have four bits of input, one bit of output.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: How many different functions are there from four bits to one bit?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: There are 16 different functions. And so you can actually just tabulate this as like 16 different, like 16 different numbers. You've got a table of 0111001, 16 entries. And so what it does is this table is stored in this blue configuration bit, and then it views these four bits as binary, looks up the relevant row of the table and emits that bit. So this is a truth table view of lookup tables essentially.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, so the lookup table if you think about an NAND gate, OR gate, NOR gate, XOR gate, these are all like take as input...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Those are like two-input functions. Sometimes we have like more complicated, like a three-input function would be a three-way XOR or a four-way XOR.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And in this case how many... it just depends how big it is? But...

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Typical size for LUTs is four input, which is sort of just a sweet spot between there's another compute communication trade-off like here. Like if it has too few inputs then you need to use more LUTs. If it has too many...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But basically the lookup table is like a truth table.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: It's a truth table, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And with a truth table you can program in any gate you want.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so I mean one of the things you can do here is you can see why the rule of thumb that an FPGA is like an order of magnitude more expensive than an ASIC comes from is to count how many gates would be inside this lookup table.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So we can view this lookup table essentially as one of these muxes and so it is a mux with has to select between 16 different values. And so it is a mux with sort of N equals 16 options, P equals one bits. And so what we saw way earlier is that this circuit costs like N times P many gates. And so it's like... so it costs like N times P equals 16 AND gates and also 16 ORs.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: This circuit being the mux?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah exactly, the mux.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The mux that goes into the lookup table.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So the lookup table itself you can think of as being actually a big mux that

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Mux selects from all 16 rows down to one output.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah, okay. That is the lookup table.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But the way you've drawn it here, there's like a mux and then a lookup table.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: It's muxes all the way down, so I mean there's a second mux that is inside here. This mux is this mux.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Got it. Okay. And then the other mux is just saying where it came from in this sort of mess of gates.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Right. And then the second mux is, okay, now you have one value, but that value is still a 4-bit value.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yes. So I've selected four bits from the soup. And then I use those four bits to select which entry in the lookup table I'm going to use.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. Okay. And just so I'm sure, suppose in the first mux there's like eight nearby... you're pulling from eight nearby registers as input. And so that's like a total of like 32 bits going in. And then out of that, four bits come out. Those four bits go into the second mux, which is inside the lookup table.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So actually I would say in this case these registers are single-bit registers. So if there are eight nearby registers and lookup tables, then I have eight bits total coming in in nearby. I select from eight down to four different values. So there's actually like four different muxes. One associated with each of these input bits. Each of them is selecting one out of eight.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And where are those eight coming from?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Nearby registers and other LUTs.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And each register is one bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so I guess AMD or whoever makes these FPGAs still has to be opinionated about what register should connect to which registers. And then you can program in the actual gates, but they had to wire in the communication topology, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So there's sort of like... you get flexibility at a local grain thing. There's a sort of nearby neighborhood where you can select from. But then more grossly, like more coarsely, longer distant connections they form an opinion on.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. Yeah. And the reason it's 10x slower is why?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So if you look at the cost of like building this lookup table, it's like 32 gates. And then it can give me the equivalent of like... what's one interesting thing I can do here? I can do a four-way AND gate. And so that's like... I'm using 32 gates of lookup table to sort of implement like a four-way AND means like what is a four-way AND? I would do like AND, AND, then AND of AND.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So to implement like this is a circuit that I could implement in an ASIC directly using these three AND gates. But using a LUT I can also implement it, but it's going to take like these 32 gates instead.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. And so the overhead is really coming from the like... the fact that the lookup table, the mux and the lookup table is... there's a more concise way to describe a truth table.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Than listing out every single possible combination of input, which is just to like write out the gate.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah, like to like place down the polysilicon and the wires and stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right, yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Interesting. One important point you made to me is that the reason they prefer FPGAs to CPUs is because they get deterministic clock cycles. They know when a packet will come in and go out. Why is that not a guarantee in CPUs?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Hmm. So you can actually design a CPU that has deterministic latency as well. And in fact, like the processes that are inside a lot of AI chips actually also have deterministic latency too. Groq has advertised this, TPUs have that in them as well. The challenge is getting sort of deterministic latency and high speed at the same time. And so where does the non-determinism in latency come from? Non-deterministic latency comes from specific design choices in a CPU. It's actually possible to remove those design choices and make a CPU that has deterministic latency. Those are not very attractive in the market, and so people don't make those CPUs anymore. But but actually in some sense, like deterministic latency is maybe a sort of a simpler designing starting point, and then and then like some chip designers have added things in to be non-deterministic. To take a concrete example of that, the probably the most important example is on a CPU just like the the CPU cache itself.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So in a CPU you have the the CPU, this is the CPU die itself, and then there's a memory off on the side. This is the DDR memory off on the side. And then you have a cache system here inside it is the cache that sort of remembers recent accesses to DDR and and stores them. And so when I'm running through my CPU instructions, every time I have an instruction that accesses memory, it first checks in cache, was the data stored in cache, and then if not it goes fetches out to DDR.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: This is a huge optimization. The cache is like two orders of magnitude faster than the DDR. If you never if you never like used the cache, basically all programs would run 100 times slower. So the presence of a cache is absolutely necessary for CPU to to run at reasonable speed. But whether or not you get a cache hit is dependent on the sort of ambient environment of the CPU, like what other programs are running, what has run recently, what is the random number generator inside the cache system doing? And so so that is a big source of non-determinism in in the runtime of a CPU. Yeah. So this sort of the memory system for a CPU.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: The the big thing that you can do differently is

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: instead of having the hardware say I'm going to like read read memory and then decide the hardware decides whether or not it comes from cash or not, you can actually bake this this decision into software.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So, a different design philosophy is is to so and you see this in maybe for example TPUs, the the TPU instead has I mean I'll draw the same diagram but I'll call it a scratch pad instead.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So the main difference is so this would be like a TPU and then HBM in this case rather than DDR but it's still an off-chip memory.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And instead of like the software saying first access like memory and then the hardware decides, you've got some instructions that go here, this is like one kind of instruction and then a totally different kind of instruction that goes to HBM.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: And so this style is generically known as scratchpad instead of cache. The key distinction being that you have like one kind of instruction that says read or write scratchpad and a totally different instruction that says read or write HBM.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So it's scratchpad being the cache.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, this thing here is the scratchpad. Just to be clear.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So stepping way back, people say computers have the quote-unquote John von Neumann architecture where there's this serial processing of information. And maybe just because we've been talking about parallel accelerators, but I just don't like FPGA is super parallel, the AI accelerator TPUs are super parallel.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Even CPUs are super parallel if you think about all the cores they have. And so is it actually, like in what sense is modern hardware actually the von Neumann architecture? Is that actually a fair way to describe modern hardware?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Um, I think it's a fair way to describe CPUs. Like just the amount of parallelism, like on a CPU the amount of parallelism you get is about 100 cores times maybe like 16-way vector units. So 106, about a thousand-way parallelism on a CPU.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I guess one question is, what is the, there is a die that is being used for the CPU, and if there's fewer threads, just as a matter of like transistor voltages or like switching on and off, is it just that there's like literally one control flow like a small part of the die where like voltages are switching on and off or like in what how do you actually occupy the die area of a CPU if there's only

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: as few cores? Like what are you spending all the die area there?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: The cores are just much bigger and more complicated. So, I mean, so I guess we should compare like a CPU core which takes up 1/100th of the die to like to a LUT, like a LUT is just only these 16 gates. So like it's clear why there are so many more LUTs in an FPGA than cores in a CPU. But then sort of maybe

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: The the like why they're more CUDA cores, for example, than CPU cores? I think that would be like why a what's the difference between a CPU and a GPU or something like that would be a big difference.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Inside the CPU you have so one big use of area in a side of CPU are the cache. Very little is actually the ALUs, like mostly it's like these register files rather than the logic units. And then the both of these things have equivalents in a GPU and so that's not a big difference. But the thing that does not have an equivalent in a GPU is the sort of this branch predictor. And so there is a whole big area in the CPU which is sort of just a whole bunch of predictors that are saying when will my next branch be and where where's the branch target for that? And so stripping a lot of that out as well as sort of making these register files tighter in a sense is driving a lot of where the like GPU gains are coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And what is the purpose of the branch predictor? To like execute both branches at once or what does it do?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: So the issue is that when I've got a series of instructions like instruction, instruction, instruction, instruction. I if I have a branch like here, if this instruction is branch, the the actual processing step of processing an instruction takes a really long amount of time. It takes like maybe five nanoseconds or something like that. So like the time to actually notice that I've got a branch and then like evaluate the boolean where whether it's true and then and then update the program counter to the new target and then read from the instruction memory for that. That that could take like like actually five nanoseconds to to finish. And so in reality, this may finish somewhere down here. I don't want to like but like I want to run at clock speed that is much faster than what five nanoseconds allows. Like like five nanoseconds is 200 megahertz clock speed. I would like to run at one or two gigahertz or something like that. And so I need to run other instructions while the branch is is being evaluated. And so I like I really just want to keep running the following instructions that happen after me. But that might have been wrong. Like if the branch ended up being taken and I need to know that instead of evaluating these instructions, I actually need to like jump to wherever the target is and and run run these instructions instead. And so the purpose of the branch predictor is like like genuinely to predict, but like before you even get to this instruction to be like five cycles earlier to predict there was going to be a branch that's going to happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So if I think about how the brain works versus what you're describing here, at a high level, the differences might be that while you can do structured sparsity in these accelerators.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: and then save yourself some area that you would have otherwise had to dedicate to these gates. In the brain, there's unstructured sparsity, you know, any neuron can connect to any other neuron and not in like ways where it has the column aligned or whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Then there's the fact that memory and computer co-located. I guess you could say in a way the memory and computer co-located on—

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: This is exactly the co-location in some sense of the memory. That's right. That's right. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yes, so maybe that actually isn't a big difference. And the other maybe a bigger difference is that the clock cycle on the brain is much slower than on on computers. And partly that's to preserve energy because the faster the clock cycle, the bigger the voltage needs to be in order to identify for the signal to settle and to like identify what state of transistor is then.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: That's right. That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I don't know if you have other high-level thoughts about like how any commentary on what the brain might be doing versus how these chips work.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, so so let's take the clock speed one first, actually. The clock speed is quite high on a chip because that drives high throughput. Like when we compare a GPU running some workload, it's running batch size 1000 or something like that, whereas like the brain is not running batch size 1000. There's only one of me. And so you could sort of imagine saying, well take a GPU and like instead of running it at a gigahertz, run it at a megahertz or something like that, and that would start to look maybe a little bit more like equivalent things that you're talking about in the brain. There is in the way that silicon works, that does not give you a thousand x advantage in energy efficiency. So what it ends up looking like is you can sort of just end up running this circuit once to stabilization and then it'll sit idle for a long period of time. It doesn't consume a lot of energy while it's sitting idle because most of the energy is consumed in sort of toggling bits from zero to one and back. Actually let's let's talk about the energy consumption of a circuit like this. The way to think of a bit being stored is you've effectively deposited some charge in a capacitor somewhere sitting somewhere in the chip implicitly. So it becomes charged when it becomes a one and then it becomes discharged when it next goes to a zero. And that cycle of like charging the capacitor and then dumping that charge out to ground, that is where the energy is consumed.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: This is called the dynamic or switching power, this is most of the energy consumption of a chip. There is some other energy consumption just coming from the fact that insulators aren't perfect insulators, but we'll dismiss that.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Just count that. Most of the energy consumption actually comes from just the charging and discharging of like toggling from zero to one and back to zero. Right. Um, so if you run a chip much slower and you only clock it once every thousand clock cycles or something, you will have a thousand times fewer transitions. It'll be about a thousand times less energy consumption, but but like, but not a substantial advantage in energy efficiency.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Um, okay, so you've described how a TPU works at a high level. Um, what is the difference at a high level between how a GPU and a TPU work?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I mean I think there's sort of a high-level organization principle that is different. Um, and then there's sort of inside the core is what are different. But we'll look at sort of outside the, uh, like at the high level. Um, so we'll take a GPU and a TPU and what does like sort of the top-level block structure look like? Um, if you think of this as the whole chip in each case, um, the organization of the GPU is mostly a bunch of almost identical units which are these, these are the SMs. Um, and then they've got an L2 memory in the middle and then a bunch more of these SMs on the bottom. Um, and so there's sort of this fairly regular grid of cores. Um, and then like if we look at a TPU in comparison, um, you you start, you end up much, with much coarser grained units of logic and so you end up with something like um, a large number of, maybe just a few matrix units, these are the, these are the big like systolic arrays, and then in the middle you've got some vector unit and then you've got your matrix units at the bottom. So, um, now, sort of matrix units with a vector unit in the middle, sort of this is the whole TPU chip. You can sort of think of taking scaling this thing down into a really tiny unit with a smaller matrix unit, smaller vector unit and that is sort of what an SM is. So, sort of at a very high level point of view, the GPU has a lot of tiny tiny TPUs sort of tiled across the whole whole chip.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Oh interesting. So like, you're suggesting the tensor core within a streaming SM is analogous or an MXU?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's very very similar, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I see. And so if you had more like, more lack of structure, having a bunch of tiny TPUs makes a lot of sense, whereas if you kind of just have like huge matrix multiplications, you're like why don't we just, why don't we avoid the cost of having the individual SMs with their own registers and warp schedulers and things like that? Why don't we just like make a huge thing and like amortize those costs?

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: across the whole thing. And I mean, I think this shows up in how large you can grow things. We've sort of seen this theme, like especially with a systolic array, where a larger systolic array amortizes the register file costs better. This sort of design allows you to have larger systolic arrays, whereas the sort of GPU design constrains you to have small units of everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: There is a trade-off, however. There ends up being, because of this sort of coarse-grained separation of things there, you need to move a lot of data from the vector unit to the matrix units. And so, like, you need to move a lot of data through, sort of, like, two lines of perimeter here. Whereas if you sort of look at the equivalent thing here, you've got vector units everywhere, and you need to move data through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line, through this line. So the amount of data you can move between a vector unit and a matrix unit is actually much higher in a GPU than in a TPU because, like, instead of having to move all the data through these just two lines, you're moving all these data through, like, 16 lines or something of wiring instead in a GPU.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right, but also you might have to move across less area.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Which, I mean, is also a saving, like, an energy saving. So data ends up moving, like, if you can operate entirely within an SM, the data movement is much smaller, but then the moment you want to operate across SMs, it becomes sort of more complicated and expensive.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So, you didn't comment, but one might expect that a thing MatX might try to do is to get the GPU-like smaller structure of systolic arrays surrounded by SRAM, but also at the same time make it so that, like, the things you need in an SM to support the CUDA architecture, but take up a bunch of space, you might discard.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Yeah. We've talked publicly about something which we call a splittable systolic array, which is sort of, in some sense, you can think of as like big systolic arrays that can be small systolic arrays as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay, I think this is a good note to close on. Reiner, thank you so much.

full_transcript.speaker: Reiner Pope
full_transcript.text: Thanks, Dwarkesh.

highlights: The fundamental primitive of AI computation is the "multiply-accumulate" (MAC) operation, which allows chips to efficiently process the nested loops required for large-scale matrix multiplication.
Moving data between a chip's register file and its logic units is often significantly more expensive in terms of die area and energy than the actual mathematical calculations themselves.
To maximize efficiency, AI chips use systolic arrays that store weight matrices locally in hardware, allowing for a massive amount of computation to occur per trip to the register file.
Chip performance scales quadratically with bit width, meaning that reducing precision from FP8 to FP4 allows for a disproportionately large increase in throughput and energy efficiency.

Designers face a constant trade-off between clock speed and area efficiency; higher frequencies require more "pipeline registers" for synchronization, which eventually consumes space that could have been used for more compute logic.
summary:
This transcript features a technical discussion between Dwarkesh Patel and Reiner Pope, CEO of MatX, regarding the internal logic and architectural design of AI chips. The conversation begins with the fundamental building blocks of hardware—logic gates and wires—before focusing on the "multiply-accumulate" (MAC) operation, which serves as the primary primitive for matrix multiplication. Pope explains that the area required for these circuits scales quadratically with bit precision, which is why lower-precision formats like FP4 offer such significant performance gains over FP8 or higher-precision alternatives.

A central theme of the discussion is the inherent tension between computation and communication. Pope demonstrates that in traditional CPU and early GPU designs, the "tax" of moving data from register files to logic units via multiplexers (muxes) often consumes more die area and energy than the math itself. To solve this, modern AI chips utilize systolic arrays (or tensor cores), which move computation up the hierarchy to matrix-vector levels. By storing weights locally within the array and "trickle-feeding" data, these designs drastically reduce the bandwidth required from expensive register files, allowing for much higher compute density.

The final section explores broader architectural trade-offs, including clock synchronization and the differences between specialized hardware. Pope describes how clock cycles act as a global synchronization mechanism, where designers must balance high frequencies against the area overhead of "pipeline registers." They contrast the flexibility and deterministic latency of FPGAs—which use lookup tables to emulate any logic gate—with the high-throughput, fixed-function nature of ASICs. Finally, they compare the "many small units" approach of GPUs to the "massive coarse-grained units" of TPUs, noting that MatX aims to find a middle ground that maximizes efficiency by stripping away the legacy overhead found in general-purpose architectures.
